---
date: 2026-06-16
title: An introduction to health and care data tools
categories: [taster sessions, pre-beginner]
---

## Introduction

This session is a quickfire, non-technical introduction to the main tools used to manage, analyse, and visualise data in current use in health and social care. Over the session, we'll introduce and demonstrate tools including Excel, Power BI, R, SQL, Tableau, and a selection of M365/Power Platform tools.

## A word of warning

Many of the tools demonstrated here will need proper governance, and most organisations will have restrictions on which tools are used, and for what. Don't take any of this demonstration as an endorsement that a tool is suitable for use in your organisation.

## Forms

* an easy tool for collecting data from users
* you can collect anonymous or identified data
* we'll use Forms to generate some sample data for this session, which will then get passed into other tools
* we'll design some questions
* please follow this link, and respond to the Form: [https://forms.office.com/e/qDU9ee7pSS](https://forms.office.com/e/qDU9ee7pSS)

## Power Automate

* a tool for process automation
* e.g. say you want to send a Teams message whenever someone replies to your Form, PA will do it for you
* ☠️governance☠️
* available to users with E5 licences

## Excel

* one advantage of Forms is that it stores its data in the most commonly-used data tool: Excel
* ubiquitous, available in several versions, and you almost certainly have access to it already
* general-purpose data storage and analysis tool
* easy to learn, and great for simple tasks
* but very few guardrails, and tends to require lots of manual management of data

## PowerQuery

* specialist data-wrangling tool
* built-in to newer versions of Excel (and Power Automate)
* helps cut-down the amount of manual work required in Excel
* harder to learn than Excel, but definitely pays for itself when the work becomes more complicated
* allows you to use friendly-ordinary Excel to import and share your data, with proper clout behind the scenes for doing the work

## Power BI

* take an Excel spreadsheet (or other data source) and visualise it
* build interactive visuals that a user can fiddle with
* hub together your data, and prevent your colleagues breaking your spreadsheets
* definitely harder to learn than Excel, but does lots of new and valuable things
* ☠️governance, and licencing is complicated, especially if you want to share with users outwith your organisation☠️

## Tableau

* similar to Power BI as a tool for building interactive dashboards
* much nicer publication options
* less capable data handling
* will almost certainly require buying licences

## R

* open-source and code-based tool for statistical work
* can do almost anything (e.g. our training website is built using R and Quarto)
* specialist statistical programming language that can also do lots of other useful tasks
* definitely harder to learn than Excel etc, but much more powerful and handy when things become more complicated

## Python

* similar code-based way of doing data work repeatably
* properly general-purpose programming language that has lots of statistical tools available
* faster than R, and with more niche options available (larger user base etc)

(won't do a live demo, because it looks very similar to R and causes all manner of trouble)

## SQL

* specialist tool for managing complex databases
* code-based
* can be used alone, or accessed with R/Python/Power BI
* very efficient storage and querying of big datasets
* lots of different options - also underpins many clinical/specialist systems
